title: Great place to start out, lots of room to grow
body: Great place to start out, lots of room to grow
pros: Lots of talented and smart people, great benefits, lots of room to grow and learn from others. Kinda easy to coast but there's also lots of pushing to grow and see results. Great place to advance, learn a lot, and be around smart people. Also really nice buildings and great food.
cons: Leadership is increasingly perceived as not listening to typical rank-and-file employees. Quiet layoffs throughout the past few years (a team here, some managers there, a re-org causing a few people to be laid off, etc.) has put a damper on morale. I was also laid off which is disappointing since my team and position in Google Cloud was focused on operations, efficiency, and cost-savings. Not completely sure of the decisions to continue laying people off when there is so much work to do which stretches remaining employees even more.

title: Still the best place to work
body: Still the best place to work
pros: Top compensation, conditions, work life balance and stability. Lots of growth opportunities. Great, really positive and really professional atmosphere. People are actually measured by their professional competence. There are weirdos like in any large corp of course, but really those are exception, not the rule. The majority of the company is not slacking off, but also not overworking to death, actually enjoying their work. Chances are you actually are going to work on some important world changing technology, I know it sounds like a corp slogan but this one is true.
cons: You can hear old timers always complain about working conditions slowly deteriorating over the years, and objectively this is true. The management very obviously is trying to push the limits. However, these changes are extremely slow, and mostly in small things, objectively working conditions are still great. Maybe it will continue like that and eventually Google will turn into another SW sweatshop, maybe not, either way it has at least a couple more good decades ahead.

title: Great place to grow technically, with some room for better strategic direction
body: Great place to grow technically, with some room for better strategic direction
pros: - Supportive and collaborative engineering team that values learning and knowledge sharing. - Flexible work arrangements and a good work-life balance. - Exposure to modern tech stacks and opportunities to work on interesting, large-scale projects. - Strong emphasis on continuous improvement and engineering best practices.
cons: - Sometimes priorities shift quickly without clear rationale, which can affect progress. - Cross-department communication could a little bit be improved, especially during product launches. - The decision-making process for larger projects can feel a bit top-down.

title: Positive Review
pros: Work with brilliant minds: Opportunity to collaborate with some of the brightest engineers, analysts, and researchers in the tech industry on cutting-edge machine learning and statistical problems Technical product managers: PMs at Google are more technical than at other companies, meaning fewer requests for basic SQL queries or spreadsheet updates that should be PM responsibilities
cons: Difficult to make individual impact: At such a massive company, it can be challenging to see how your individual contributions meaningfully affect the business or product Slow product release cycles: Despite working like startups, teams often face sluggish release cycles with long hours but without the agility and rapid iteration of actual startups

title: High Impact, Higher Red Tape
pros: Having Google on your CV is a career cheat code. It guarantees interviews for the rest of your life
cons: You will inevitably spend six months on a project only for it to be axed by a VP you've never met. Your hard work will likely end up in a graveyard of killed products

title: Great place to start from
pros: Google offers an incredible environment for engineers to work on cutting-edge technology at massive scale. The learning opportunities are unparalleled - access to brilliant colleagues, world-class infrastructure, and resources to tackle complex problems. The work culture emphasizes innovation, collaboration, and work-life balance. Excellent compensation and benefits package, including health insurance, learning budgets, and career development programs. You get to work on products that impact billions of users while having the freedom to explore new ideas and technologies.
cons: The sheer size of the organization can sometimes make processes feel slow. Navigating internal systems and getting alignment across teams can be challenging. Promotion timelines can feel long, and high competition means standing out requires consistent exceptional performance. Some teams may have less autonomy compared to smaller companies or startups.

title: Not quite 'just another big company.' Yet. 3.5 stars.
body: Not quite 'just another big company.' Yet. 3.5 stars.
pros: There's a lot of good stuff to work on, fantastic tooling, and less tech debt than most places of similar size. Colleagues are usually very skilled and smart.
cons: There was a time when Google had an unusually good culture of openness, technical excellence, doing the right thing, and not letting all the usual bureaucratic turf wars bog it down. That time has entirely passed. Getting to work on the good stuff is a matter of politics over suitability, performance reviews have gained the bad parts of the standard methods and kept the bad parts of the idiosyncratic methods, and you *will* face turf wars that get in the way of doing your job. Also the strategy to avoid tech debt is 'reimplement everything after 5 years or kill it' which has always had its drawbacks.

cons: Honestly, it's a very comfortable place. So, if you're craving something more than comfort or that's the anti-thesis of comfort (or requires you to get get outside of your comfort-zone), you might want to look somewhere else. That said, if what you're optimizing for is a comfortable, high-paying, high-prestige job, I'm not sure that it gets any better than this.

title: Political nonsense; Toxic leadership
pros: Name recognition though these days no one seems to care about Google brand. Decent comp if you work in the premium areas like Bay or NYC. Good benefits.
cons: Cloud is a political nightmare with half the leaders from Oracle and AWS. Extremely toxic leadership in Cloud. The good old google is long gone. Lack of diversity with mostly Indians and Chinese. Every PA is outsourcing the roles to India offices. Grad which is their performance process is a joke with rigged outcomes and no way to appeal. My experience at Google has been terrible with lack of leadership and mediocre VPs and Senior Director's trying to grow their empires with no vision.
role: Current employee - Senior Staff Engineer, Software

title: Was great for 8-9 years, bad for the last year and a half
body: Was great for 8-9 years, bad for the last year and a half
pros: Solid work life balance, great amenities, great office, some really great coworkers
cons: When stock is down, the company stops caring about your well-being. It's becoming more of a typical corporation - caring about profits over people, encouraging less work-life balance, a lot less perks, a 'hustle' culture where if you're not constantly promoting yourself or trying to get promoted they will try to boot you out and generally a shift in the culture that many other employees and former employees I've spoken to describe as toxic.

body: Benefits/Pay remain competitive, but Culture has declined

cons: However, the differentiating culture of Google that had attracted so many initially is largely gone. The goodwill is no longer there, people are stressed and competitive about the next role, and there's probably not much to differentiate it now from any other tech company. Upward mobility is limited.

title: Strong compensation, world-class peers, but pace and ambiguity arenât for everyone
body: Strong compensation, world-class peers, but pace and ambiguity arenât for everyone
pros: Compensation and benefits are among the best in the industry, especially once you factor in equity and refreshers. You work with very smart, driven engineers; code quality expectations are high. Strong internal tooling, documentation, and infraâmost problems already have a scalable solution somewhere. Generally good workâlife balance compared to other big tech, depending heavily on team. Brand name opens doors later in your career.
cons: Impact can feel diluted. Projects are large, and individual contributions may take time to show results. Internal processes can be slow and bureaucratic; getting consensus often matters as much as technical merit. Performance reviews and promotion criteria can feel opaque and political, especially at mid to senior levels. Team experience varies widely - your day-to-day quality of life depends a lot on your manager and org. Frequent reorgs can derail long-term work.

title: Great place to work
pros: Working at Google offers an exceptional opportunity to collaborate with brilliant minds on massive-scale technical challenges while enjoying industry-leading benefits and campus amenities. However, navigating the bureaucracy of such a large organization can be slow, making it difficult at times to see the direct impact of your individual contributions on final products.
cons: The sheer size of the organization often leads to sluggish bureaucracy and "red tape," making it difficult for individuals to ship products quickly or feel a strong sense of ownership. Furthermore, the internal competition for promotions and the frequent cancellation of projects can make career progression feel more political than merit-based.

pros: Open culture, they have been adding more hierarchy but still you can have an open conversation with directors or VPs. Work life balance still though that has been changing a bit.
cons: Google has been trying to maintain work life balance culture but with the current tech AI race the pressure is on, especially in sales org - crazy targets, frequent lay-offs. You constantly feeling like you are on the edge. Expectations have sky-rocketed like never before. Internal mobility is there but limited. No career growth and very few promos.

title: Good benefits but often boring work
body: Good benefits but often boring work
pros: Good benefits, competitive salary on a country level, good work life balance
cons: Work can be boring, especially in low-paid offices. They move old projects from US to cheaper location so that more engineers can maintain them. Salary is competitive but only if compared with local jobs. In Warsaw, salary is much lower than in Zurich even though you often work with them. Attitude of many engineers is "do 2 years here, move to Zurich / US, etc" which is demotivating. Some managers are hired quickly and don't seem to be very skilled.

title: Good people, very corporate
pros: Google isn't the same place it was 10 years ago, but you're still surrounded by ultra smart, motivated people. For the most part the people you work with are friendly and helpful, and you have access to resources that most people can only dream about.
cons: All the machinery around you is ultra corporate and getting more so by the day, from perf processes to using unapproved software on your machine. Also, everything is done with ultra bespoke frameworks and tools that are made for handling way more traffic than you'll likely need, so doing anything like launching an internal web app is a huge pain.

title: Good and Bad
pros: I enjoyed my time at Google. The main upside is the people. Working with sharp people who really care is a wonderful thing, not to be underestimated.
cons: Google's infra and software stack complicates a lot of tasks, which can be discouraging and if you're the kind of person who is easily affected by this kind of friction it may be wise to consider another opportunity, if available. I found that it was hard to maintain velocity. Sometimes it was the company's fault, sometimes it was my fault, but at the end of the day it ended up not being the right place for me long-term.

title: Good Review
pros: Strong work culture & learning environment â Google is known for a collaborative engineering culture with lots of internal tech talks, learning opportunities, and exposure to cutting-edge technology. High compensation and benefits â Salaries and stock packages tend to be very competitive in the industry, with perks like free food, wellness programs, and flexible working arrangements. Innovative and impactful work â You get to work on products used by millions or billions; many developers value that impact. Diversity and inclusion are generally rated well among employees.
cons: Experience varies a lot by team â Some teams (especially AI or infrastructure teams) can have high pressure and long hours, while others are more balanced. Promotion/process complexity â Several reviewers say career progression or performance reviews can feel opaque or bureaucratic. Internal politics & bureaucracy â Typical for large organizations; some feel slowed down by meetings and approval layers.

title: nice
pros: Exceptional Perks & Benefits: Free food, gyms, great healthcare, and other amenities significantly boost employee well-being. Innovation & Impact: Work on cutting-edge projects with potential to affect billions, alongside brilliant minds. Collaborative Culture: An open, inclusive environment encourages idea sharing and teamwork. High Compensation: Competitive salaries and stock options are standard, especially for top talent. Flexibility & Autonomy: Often allows for flexible hours, focusing on results rather than rigid 9-to-5, with room for creative freedom.
cons: Intense Pressure & Competition: High performance expectations and internal competition can lead to stress and burnout. Bureaucracy & Slow Decisions: Large size means slow approvals and complex project management, despite autonomy. Career Progression Challenges: Promotions can be slow, political, and difficult to navigate, even with strong mentorship. Constant Skill Upkeep: The rapid pace of tech requires continuous learning to stay relevant, which can be demanding. Limited Scope/Role Stagnation: Some employees feel their specific role might have a limited impact or get stuck in large systems.

title: One of the worst company to work for
body: One of the worst company to work for
pros: They serve food so if you can take out 30 mins from your day to go and get something to eat, you don't need to go far
cons: Google was once one of the best companies to work for but now it's probably one of the worst. Toxic work culture and managers who have no boundaries. Managers call their reportees in the middle of the night and during weekends on their cell phone. Meetings that are supposed to be an hour long, frequently continue for 2-2.5 hrs on demand of any of the senior leaders. Barely any break during the day to even eat or go to the washroom. Working for any company is better than working for Google today if you want to have a life outside work. This is specific to Google cloud.

title: A great place to grow, learn, and solve meaningful problems
body: A great place to grow, learn, and solve meaningful problems
pros: Google offers an exceptional engineering culture with strong emphasis on learning, innovation, and collaboration. The technical challenges are meaningful, large-scale, and intellectually stimulating. Workâlife balance is generally good depending on the team, and the benefits, from compensation to health and wellness resources, are among the best in the industry. The company also provides excellent opportunities for career development and internal mobility.
cons: Decision-making can be slow due to the companyâs large size and multiple layers of approval. Some projects move at a slower pace than expected, and navigating internal tools and processes can be overwhelming. There can also be significant variation between teams in terms of workload, expectations, and culture. Promotion processes may feel competitive and highly structured.

title: nnovative environment with brilliant peers, but growth can feel unstructured
body: nnovative environment with brilliant peers, but growth can feel unstructured
pros: Cutting-edge projects: You get to work on products used by billions. The scale and impact are unmatched. Brilliant colleagues: The hiring bar is high, so youâre surrounded by smart, passionate people who push you to grow. Outstanding benefits: From free gourmet meals and onsite wellness centers to generous parental leave and 401(k) matchingâthe perks are industry-leading. Flexibility & autonomy: Managers generally trust you to manage your time and projects. Hybrid/remote options are widely available post-pandemic. Learning culture: Internal talks, workshops, and access to resources like Coursera keep you constantly learning.
cons: Promotions can be opaque: Advancement often feels political or dependent on visibility rather than pure technical skill. The perf (performance review) process is stressful and sometimes arbitrary. Growing bureaucracy: As Google scales, decision-making has slowed. More layers of approval and âalignment meetingsâ than in the past. Team dependence: Your experience varies wildly by team. Some teams have great WLB, others expect late nights and weekendsâespecially in cloud or ads. Competitive internal mobility: Transferring to a desired team/project can be surprisingly competitive and time-consuming. Feeling like a cog: On larger products, itâs easy to feel removed from the end-user impact.

title: Most of what you've heard is true!
body: Most of what you've heard is true!
pros: Working at Google is great, for all the well-documented reasons that you've already heard. Excellent culture, great work-life-balance, tons of learning opportunities, work that's both rewarding and challenging, and generally competitive compensation, perks, and benefits.
cons: Post-pandemic recovery has been hard on company culture. Many of the old guard have begun their early retirements and/or moved on to start their own ventures, so there's some risk from shifting institutional memory. Promotions to Level 6 and beyond are becoming increasingly rare. Perception of executives is generally not as favorable as it once was. If you're seeking a nimble, ultra-fast-paced startup environment, Google hasn't been this for well over a decade and you should look elsewhere; despite this, the speed and effectiveness of this company and its people can be impressive.
